---
title: "Ontologies, kept small"
description: "An ontology is a formal vocabulary for a domain; real ones are often large. This project's stance is the opposite, keep it small, because that is what makes an agent's reasoning reliable."
date: "2026-05-21"
categories: [graph]
---

**What an ontology is.** An ontology is a formal specification of the concepts in a domain: the kinds of things that exist, how they relate, and the constraints on them. It is a long knowledge-representation tradition with a W3C authoring standard, **OWL**, and large real examples you may already use, [schema.org](https://schema.org) (the shared vocabulary search engines read off web pages) and the Gene Ontology (millions of biology annotations). Real ontologies are frequently big and heavyweight.

Its companion for validation is **SHACL** (Shapes Constraint Language), also a W3C standard. You declare *shapes*, for instance "a page of `type: source` must have exactly one `source:` field, and it must be a URL", and SHACL checks a data graph against those shapes and emits a pass/fail report. It is a schema-validator for graphs.

**Our stance: keep it small.** Against that heavyweight backdrop, this project deliberately goes the other way. The wiki's graph is grounded in a small ontology (the [la3d/llm-wiki-colab](https://la3d.github.io/llm-wiki-colab/) vocabulary) with SHACL over it, and the argument for keeping it small is a reliability one. An LLM reasoning over an explicit small ontology is accurate; an LLM inferring structure from raw, unlabelled data is measurably less accurate and often needs a human to catch mistakes. So the reliable path is not "make the agent clever enough to guess at undescribed sources"; it is the reverse: give every source a small ontology, cheap to author, and let the agent reason over that. A heavy ontology over-commits provisional structure and returns over-confident results; a small one is what makes reasoning trustworthy.

That is also the connector contract: a source is not integrated when you can call its API, but when it is described well enough for an agent to reason over without guessing.

## Related

- [The wiki is a knowledge graph](../knowledge-graphs/), what the ontology structures.
- [Connectors are agents](../connectors/), where a per-source ontology earns its keep.
- [Wiki front matter](../wiki-front-matter/), the typed edges the ontology names.
